items.headline: Beijing 2026 Book Fair Focuses on Publishing Transformation, AI, and the Future of Reading
items.excerpt: The 2026 Beijing International Book Fair centered on AI's transformation of publishing — from AI-assisted editing and rights trading to AI-powered reader personalization — with Chinese publishers showcasing aggressive AI integration across the production chain. Western delegates noted the speed gap and debated whether opt-in licensing frameworks can keep pace.

items.headline: AI Already Embedded in Publishing in China, Beijing International Book Fair Hears
items.excerpt: The Beijing International Book Fair heard first-hand accounts of AI being deeply embedded across China's publishing industry — in translation, editing, rights management, and customer personalization — while Western publishers remained in cautious pilot mode. The contrast opened a frank debate about competitive disadvantage for traditional publishers.

items.headline: Plutonium or Salt? When It Comes to Books, How Much AI Is Too Much?
items.excerpt: Publishing Perspectives asks where publishers draw the line on AI integration — whether AI functions as a trace element that enhances the human process or a contaminant that transforms what a book fundamentally is. Editors, agents, and authors share their personal thresholds in a candid industry survey.

items.headline: Links of Interest: AI and Publishing — What Writers Need to Know This Week (July 1, 2026)
items.excerpt: Jane Friedman's weekly curated roundup covers the most important AI and publishing developments for working writers — from new disclosure policies and contract clauses to avoid, to the emerging market for human-verified creative work. A practical primer for navigating AI's accelerating impact on the writing and publishing business.

items.headline: Authors Guild Speaks Out on Publisher AI Use
items.excerpt: The Authors Guild has issued a formal statement criticizing publishers for adopting AI tools that affect authors' work without transparency or meaningful consent, demanding clearer contract protections and revenue-sharing arrangements for AI-assisted projects. The statement follows reports that major publishers are deploying AI quietly in editorial, marketing, and rights processes.
